scenario: A finance-processing workflow has a critical transactional section of 3 Service Tasks: reserve-funds, transfer-funds, confirm-transfer. The team needs strict transactional semantics: either all 3 succeed and the section commits, or all 3 are rolled back via compensation if any fails. The team is considering using the BPMN Transaction Subprocess construct.
question: Which BPMN construct expresses transactional semantics?

options.text: A Transaction Subprocess (a specific Subprocess type rendered with a double border) containing the 3 tasks. Inside the transaction, each reversible task has a Compensation Boundary Event with its handler. A Cancel End Event inside the transaction triggers a Cancel Boundary Event on the transaction; the cancel propagates compensation to all attached handlers. md:47 48, 570 577 (none of the three symbols carries className="implemented"). The construct is modeler visible only and Zeebe rejects it at deployment.

Three separate Service Tasks wired with explicit reverse path flows -- each reversible task has a dedicated rollback task connected via conditional sequence flows. Workable in theory: if reserve funds fails, route to release funds; if transfer funds fails, route to reverse transfer. " The BPMN compensation event family exists precisely to handle this pattern idiomatically.

Camunda 8 has no mechanism for transactional rollback of any kind -- teams must handle rollback entirely through application code outside the BPMN model. Teams regularly use this pattern for saga style rollback orchestration.
md:32 34). md:73 83. This is the canonical C8 "saga / transactional rollback" pattern in absence of Transaction Subprocess.

a.text: Incorrect. Transaction Subprocess, Cancel End, and Cancel Boundary are BPMN-spec elements but not implemented in Camunda 8 per bpmn-coverage.md:47-48, 570-577. They are modeler-visible only (you can draw them, but Zeebe rejects them at deployment / does not execute them). Selecting this construct gives an unrunnable model.

b.text: Incorrect. Per bpmn-coverage.md the Transaction Subprocess symbol (line 48) has no className="implemented"; same for Cancel Boundary (line 570) and Cancel End (line 577). The C8-supported way to express "all 3 succeed or all rolled back via compensation" is therefore: wrap the 3 tasks in an Embedded Subprocess; attach a Compensation Boundary Event + handler to each reversible inner task; on the failure path, place a Compensation End Event (or Compensation Intermediate Throw). Per compensation-events.md:26-29, the throw invokes "all compensation handlers of completed activities" and remains active until all handlers complete. By default they run "at once without any specific order" (:32-34); for strict per-activity order, use multiple throws each with activityRef (:73-83).

c.text: Incorrect. Wiring 3 reverse paths manually loses the BPMN compensation abstraction (Compensation Boundary + Throw). Each path needs explicit conditional flow; readers must reverse-engineer the intent. Reuse of the same activity by both happy path and rollback is also error-prone.

d.text: Correct. The Compensation event family is fully implemented (compensation-events.md is the spec page), so transactional-style rollback IS supported -- just via Embedded + Compensation, not Transaction Subprocess.

explanation: The Compensation event family is fully implemented (compensation-events.md is the spec page), so transactional-style rollback IS supported -- just via Embedded + Compensation, not Transaction Subprocess.
